news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in ancient plant genetics, China's commitment to leading in AI, innovative uses of classic computer games for scientific research, and advancements in AI tools enhancing educational discussions.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine de la génétique des plantes anciennes, l'engagement de la Chine à devenir un leader en matière d'IA, des utilisations innovantes de jeux vidéo classiques pour la recherche scientifique, ainsi que les progrès réalisés dans les outils d'IA destinés à enrichir les débats pédagogiques.

articles.title: Scientists discover ancient DNA “switches” hidden in plants for 400 million years
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered an enormous hidden archive of plant DNA that has endured for more than 400 million years. By comparing hundreds of plant genomes, researchers identified more than 2.3 million regulatory DNA sequences that act like genetic switches, controlling when and how genes are activated. These sequences, known as conserved non-coding sequences (CNSs), were detected using a new computational tool called Conservatory.
articles.content:
Most people are familiar with the idea of deep space, but scientists also study something called deep time. Advances in genetics now allow researchers to trace biological changes far deeper into the past than previously possible. Even with these powerful tools, many questions remain unanswered. One long standing puzzle has challenged biologists for decades.

Genes and their functions often stay remarkably similar across species, even when those species diverged hundreds of millions of years ago. This pattern appears in both plants and animals. However, the same consistency does not seem to apply to the DNA that controls when genes turn on or off. Scientists have struggled to determine whether this type of DNA, known as regulatory DNA, remains conserved in plants over long evolutionary periods. For many years, some researchers believed that such conservation might not exist in plants at all. New findings suggest otherwise.

Discovery of Ancient Regulatory DNA in Plants

A study published in Science by Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory (CSHL) and collaborators around the world has identified more than 2.3 million regulatory DNA sequences that remain conserved across 314 plant genomes from 284 species. These sequences are known as conserved non-coding sequences (CNSs). The team located them using a new computational tool called Conservatory, developed through collaboration among the laboratories of Idan Efroni at Hebrew University, Madelaine Bartlett at Sainsbury Laboratory Cambridge University, and Zachary Lippman at CSHL.

Some of these CNSs appear to be extremely ancient. The researchers found evidence that certain sequences originated before flowering plants diverged from their non-flowering ancestors more than 400 million years ago.

Comparing Hundreds of Plant Genomes

How were scientists able to uncover such a large number of previously hidden regulatory sequences?

The researchers focused on examining the organization and composition of gene groups at a very small scale. By comparing how these gene clusters are arranged across hundreds of plant genomes and tracing their patterns from ancestral species to modern plants, they were able to detect conserved elements that earlier methods had missed.

CSHL postdoc Anat Hendelman, a co-first author of the study, said the team was surprised by how many of these regulatory sequences had existed unnoticed. "Picking apart and genetically editing these CNSs confirmed they're essential for developmental function," Hendelman says.

Three Key Rules of Plant Regulatory DNA Evolution

The study also revealed three important patterns that help explain how CNSs evolve in plant genomes.

First, even though the physical spacing between these sequences can change, their order along the chromosome tends to stay consistent. Second, when plant genomes are rearranged during evolution, CNSs may become linked to different genes. Third, ancient CNSs often remain present after genes are duplicated, which is a major driver of plant genome and gene family evolution.

"This was actually one reason CNSs could not be discovered using the same approaches used in animals," Lippman explains. "We didn't just find CNSs using this innovative approach. We found that new regulatory sequences often come from old CNSs that were modified after gene duplication. This helps explain how novel regulatory elements emerge."

A New Atlas for Plant Biology and Crop Science

The Conservatory project has created what researchers describe as a "comprehensive atlas of regulatory conservation across plants, including dozens of crop species and their wild ancestors." Plant biologists such as CSHL collaborator David Jackson can now use this resource to explore how regulatory DNA has been preserved and reshaped across plant evolution.

The findings may prove especially valuable for crop breeders who are trying to address challenges such as drought and food shortages. Yet the importance of the discovery extends well beyond agriculture. As Lippman puts it, "It's a new window into the evolution of life across eons and a new opportunity to more efficiently engineer or fine-tune crop traits."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in plant genomics, identifying over 2.3 million ancient regulatory DNA sequences conserved for more than 400 million years. This discovery, enabled by a novel computational tool, provides a comprehensive atlas that can advance plant biology and crop science, potentially aiding in addressing global challenges like drought and food shortages. The impact is broad, meaningful, and well-detailed, with clear implications for agriculture and evolutionary biology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find ancient genetic switches in plants dating back 400 million years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how genes work in plants, but it was unclear if the DNA that controls when genes turn on or off stays the same over very long periods. This type of DNA is called regulatory DNA.

**What happened** – Researchers from Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory and other institutions compared 314 plant genomes from 284 species. Using a new computer tool named Conservatory, they found over 2.3 million regulatory DNA sequences called conserved non-coding sequences (CNSs). Some of these CNSs are more than 400 million years old, existing before flowering plants evolved.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that plants have ancient genetic switches that control development and have stayed preserved through millions of years. It helps scientists understand how plants evolved and how these DNA switches change over time. The new atlas of these sequences includes many crop plants, offering valuable information for improving crops to face problems like drought and food shortages.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use this atlas to study how these ancient DNA switches work in different plants. Crop breeders may use this knowledge to develop better plants that grow well in tough conditions. The discovery also opens new research into how life evolved over hundreds of millions of years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers uncovered millions of ancient genetic switches in plants that have stayed almost unchanged for 400 million years, offering new tools for biology and farming.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des interrupteurs génétiques anciens dans les plantes vieux de 400 millions d'années
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps le fonctionnement des gènes chez les plantes, mais on ne savait pas si l'ADN qui contrôle l'activation des gènes reste identique sur de très longues périodes. Ce type d'ADN s'appelle l'ADN régulateur.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory et d'autres institutions ont comparé 314 génomes de plantes provenant de 284 espèces. Grâce à un nouvel outil informatique appelé Conservatory, ils ont trouvé plus de 2,3 millions de séquences d'ADN régulateur appelées séquences non codantes conservées (CNS). Certaines de ces CNS ont plus de 400 millions d'années, datant d'avant l'apparition des plantes à fleurs.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les plantes possèdent des interrupteurs génétiques anciens qui contrôlent leur développement et sont restés préservés pendant des millions d'années. Elle aide les scientifiques à comprendre l'évolution des plantes et comment ces interrupteurs changent avec le temps. L'atlas de ces séquences comprend de nombreuses plantes cultivées, offrant des informations précieuses pour améliorer les cultures face à des problèmes comme la sécheresse et la faim.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront cet atlas pour étudier le fonctionnement de ces anciens interrupteurs dans différentes plantes. Les sélectionneurs de plantes pourront s'appuyer sur ces connaissances pour développer de meilleures plantes capables de pousser dans des conditions difficiles. Cette découverte ouvre aussi de nouvelles recherches sur l'évolution de la vie sur des centaines de millions d'années.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont découvert des millions d'interrupteurs génétiques anciens chez les plantes, restés presque inchangés depuis 400 millions d'années, offrant de nouveaux outils pour la biologie et l'agriculture.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren interruptores genéticos antiguos en plantas de hace 400 millones de años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo funcionan los genes en las plantas, pero no se sabía si el ADN que controla cuándo se activan los genes se mantiene igual durante períodos muy largos. Este tipo de ADN se llama ADN regulador.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory y otras instituciones compararon 314 genomas de plantas de 284 especies. Usando una nueva herramienta informática llamada Conservatory, encontraron más de 2,3 millones de secuencias de ADN regulador llamadas secuencias no codificantes conservadas (CNS). Algunas de estas CNS tienen más de 400 millones de años, y existían antes de que evolucionaran las plantas con flores.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que las plantas tienen interruptores genéticos antiguos que controlan su desarrollo y se han mantenido preservados durante millones de años. Ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo evolucionaron las plantas y cómo cambian estos interruptores a lo largo del tiempo. El nuevo atlas de estas secuencias incluye muchas plantas cultivadas, ofreciendo información valiosa para mejorar los cultivos frente a problemas como la sequía y la escasez de alimentos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán este atlas para estudiar cómo funcionan estos antiguos interruptores en diferentes plantas. Los mejoradores de cultivos podrán usar este conocimiento para desarrollar plantas que crezcan mejor en condiciones difíciles. Este descubrimiento también abre nuevas investigaciones sobre la evolución de la vida durante cientos de millones de años.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los investigadores descubrieron millones de interruptores genéticos antiguos en plantas que han permanecido casi sin cambios durante 400 millones de años, ofreciendo nuevas herramientas para la biología y la agricultura.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of an ancient, sprawling plant genome depicted as a glowing, branching network of intertwined DNA strands and gene clusters, with delicate, translucent "switch" elements—symbolized as luminous, ornate keys or locks—embedded throughout; the background subtly fades into layers of fossilized plant silhouettes and evolving leaf shapes, illustrating 400 million years of plant evolution and the discovery of hidden regulatory DNA sequences conserved across time.

articles.title: Top brass in China reaffirm goal to be world leaders in tech, AI
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 14 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00814-3The new five-year plan calls for more original scientific research to facilitate the country's bid for self-reliance.
articles.content:
Credit: Kevin Frayer/Getty

China is pledging to use ‘extraordinary measures’ to support the country's bid to become a global leader in artificial intelligence, quantum technology and other cutting-edge technological fields, according to its 15th five-year plan.

The plan (FYP) was passed by the top legislature in Beijing on Thursday and published on Friday. It will run from 2026 to 2030 and serves as China’s overarching blueprint.

Many researchers noted an air of confidence in the plan. “Five years ago, the sentiment of the Chinese science policymakers was still very much like, we don’t want to be too far behind the US, we are still doing the catching up,” says Meicen Sun, an information scientist at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. “Now, there is this more palpable sentiment that there’s a real chance we can be a true leader,” she says.

The government has promised to boost its research and development (R&D) expenditure over the next five years. And the country’s science budget is also expected to increase to 426 billion yuan (US$62 billion) this year, a rise of 10% from 2025.

The Chinese government now considers science to be as important as other top-level national goals, such as boosting defence, economic growth and the country’s international influence, says Stefanie Kam, who researches Chinese politics at Nanyang Technological University in Singapore.

Technology strangleholds

The plan also doubles down on the government’s long-term goal of becoming more self-sufficient by overcoming technological bottlenecks that prevent China from making key technologies, such as advanced semiconductor chips.

It calls for breakthroughs along the ‘whole chain of development’ in six domains: integrated circuits, industrial machine tools (machines that make other machines), high-end instruments, basic software, advanced materials and biomanufacturing.

That essentially means that the country will step up its domestic capabilities in every aspect of those industries, says Steven Hai, a political economist focusing on technology innovation at Xi’an Jiaotong-Liverpool University in Suzhou, China.

Although China has tried to overcome technological choke points for many years, the mission has been brought to the fore in the 15th FYP, mainly owing to China–US competition for technological supremacy, says Zhou Weihuan, a legal scholar specializing in China at the University of New South Wales in Sydney, Australia.

The plan doesn’t go into detail about what the extraordinary measures will entail, but Sun suggests they will include provisions such as the ‘K visa’, which was rolled out last year to attract foreign scientists. “Extraordinary times call for extraordinary measures. This document is a prime embodiment of this,” she says.

The country will also fast-track R&D in areas such as biotechnology, neuroscience and deep-space exploration.

AI strategy

The plan doubles down on the use of artificial intelligence, applying the technology across society, in fields ranging from industrial development to social governance, as part of a national campaign called AI plus, which was announced last year.

AI research is now being treated as a crucial and strategic national resource that requires security along the whole supply chain, including chips, basic software and training to ensure mass adoption, Kam says.

In early 2025, Chinese tech start-up firm DeepSeek shocked the world by releasing two large language models (LLMs) that rivalled the performance of the dominant tools developed by US tech giants — but that were built at a fraction of the cost and computing power needed to train their US counterparts.

Sun says that the country’s AI advances have served as a confidence booster for its “attitudinal change” as to its standing in the global technological race. She expects China to not only develop AI as a technology, but also to “actively and pre-emptively” write the global rule books on AI governance and regulation.

Public and private
articles.reasoning: The article reports on China's ambitious five-year plan to become a global leader in AI, quantum technology, and other advanced fields by significantly increasing R&D investment and focusing on self-reliance in critical technologies. This plan has broad significance due to its potential impact on global technological innovation and competition, and it includes concrete measures and goals, providing substantial context and detail.
articles.personality_title: China unveils bold five-year plan to lead in AI and advanced tech
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – China has long aimed to become a top player in technology, especially in fields like artificial intelligence (AI) and quantum computing. Competition with other countries, especially the United States, has pushed China to focus more on developing its own technology without relying on others.

**What happened** – In March 2026, China’s government approved a new five-year plan that will run from 2026 to 2030. This plan promises to spend more money on scientific research and technology development. It highlights six key areas where China wants to improve, including making better computer chips, advanced machines, and software. The plan also emphasizes using AI widely across society and speeding up research in fields like biotechnology and space exploration.

**Impact** – This plan is important because it shows China’s confidence in becoming a world leader in technology. For the first time, China is openly aiming not just to catch up but to lead. The government is increasing its science budget by 10% this year, reaching $62 billion, which is a big boost. The focus on self-reliance means China wants to solve problems that have kept it from making some advanced products on its own. The plan’s attention to AI also reflects China’s goal to shape the future rules for how AI is used worldwide.

**What’s next step** – Over the next five years, China will work to reach the goals set in this plan. This includes encouraging foreign scientists to come work in China and speeding up research projects in key technology areas. Experts expect China to continue growing its AI capabilities and to play a bigger role in setting global standards for technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – China’s new five-year plan sets a clear path to boost science and technology, aiming to become a global leader in AI and other advanced fields by 2030.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Chine dévoile un plan quinquennal ambitieux pour dominer l’IA et la haute technologie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Chine cherche depuis longtemps à devenir un acteur majeur de la technologie, notamment dans l’intelligence artificielle (IA) et l’informatique quantique. La concurrence avec d’autres pays, notamment les États-Unis, a poussé la Chine à développer ses propres technologies sans dépendre des autres.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En mars 2026, le gouvernement chinois a approuvé un nouveau plan quinquennal qui s’étendra de 2026 à 2030. Ce plan promet d’augmenter les dépenses en recherche scientifique et en développement technologique. Il met en avant six domaines clés où la Chine veut progresser, comme la fabrication de puces informatiques, de machines avancées et de logiciels. Le plan insiste aussi sur l’utilisation de l’IA dans toute la société et l’accélération de la recherche en biotechnologie et exploration spatiale.

**Impact** – Ce plan est important car il montre la confiance de la Chine à devenir un leader mondial en technologie. Pour la première fois, la Chine vise non seulement à rattraper son retard, mais à prendre la tête. Le budget scientifique augmente de 10 % cette année, atteignant 62 milliards de dollars, une hausse importante. L’accent mis sur l’autonomie signifie que la Chine veut résoudre les problèmes qui l’empêchaient de fabriquer certains produits avancés elle-même. L’attention portée à l’IA reflète aussi la volonté de la Chine de définir les règles futures pour son usage dans le monde.

**Prochaine étape** – Dans les cinq prochaines années, la Chine va travailler à atteindre les objectifs du plan. Cela inclut d’attirer des scientifiques étrangers et d’accélérer les projets de recherche dans les domaines technologiques clés. Les experts s’attendent à ce que la Chine renforce ses capacités en IA et joue un rôle plus important dans la définition des normes mondiales.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau plan quinquennal de la Chine trace une voie claire pour renforcer la science et la technologie, visant à devenir un leader mondial en IA et autres domaines avancés d’ici 2030.
articles.personality_title_es: China presenta un ambicioso plan quinquenal para liderar en IA y tecnología avanzada
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – China ha buscado durante mucho tiempo ser un líder en tecnología, especialmente en inteligencia artificial (IA) y computación cuántica. La competencia con otros países, especialmente Estados Unidos, ha motivado a China a desarrollar su propia tecnología sin depender de otros.

**Qué pasó** – En marzo de 2026, el gobierno chino aprobó un nuevo plan quinquenal que cubrirá de 2026 a 2030. Este plan promete aumentar la inversión en investigación científica y desarrollo tecnológico. Destaca seis áreas clave donde China quiere mejorar, como la fabricación de chips, máquinas avanzadas y software. También enfatiza el uso de IA en toda la sociedad y acelerar la investigación en biotecnología y exploración espacial.

**Impacto** – Este plan es importante porque muestra la confianza de China para convertirse en líder mundial en tecnología. Por primera vez, China no solo quiere alcanzar a otros, sino liderar. El presupuesto para ciencia aumentará un 10% este año, llegando a 62 mil millones de dólares, un gran impulso. El enfoque en la autosuficiencia significa que China quiere resolver problemas que le han impedido fabricar algunos productos avanzados por sí misma. La atención en IA también refleja el objetivo de China de influir en las reglas globales para su uso.

**Próximo paso** – En los próximos cinco años, China trabajará para cumplir las metas del plan. Esto incluye atraer científicos extranjeros y acelerar proyectos de investigación en áreas tecnológicas clave. Se espera que China continúe desarrollando sus capacidades en IA y tenga un papel más importante en establecer normas globales.

**Frase clave** – El nuevo plan quinquenal de China establece un camino claro para impulsar la ciencia y tecnología, con el objetivo de ser líder mundial en IA y otras áreas avanzadas para 2030.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a majestic, stylized dragon coiled protectively around glowing, interconnected circuit patterns and quantum symbols, set against a backdrop of rising gears and delicate neural network branches, all rendered in warm, natural tones to symbolize China’s confident, forward-looking embrace of AI, quantum technology, and self-reliant innovation.

articles.title: How the classic computer game Doom became a tool for science
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00813-4The 1990s game has been run on bacteria and a satellite and played by neurons in a dish.
articles.content:
Developers of the computer game Doom released the game's code in 1997, allowing scientists to use it as part of their research.Credit: id Software via ArcadeImages/Alamy

When the computer game Doom was released in 1993, its utility for science wasn’t immediately clear. Since then, the first-person shooter has been used in many studies, from helping to improve artificial-intelligence models1 to investigating the effects of video games on memory and aggression2.

It has also spawned a subculture in which fans and developers, including scientists, try to run the game on different devices — from calculators to digital pregnancy tests. Last month, scientists in Australia reported that they had taught neurons grown on a silicon chip to play the game. The phrases ‘Can it run Doom?’ and ‘It runs Doom’ have become a popular Internet meme.

Alon Loeffler, a synthetic-biological-intelligence scientist who was part of the team at biotechnology company Cortical Labs in Melbourne, Australia, which trained the neurons, says the team chose Doom because of the meme. He and his colleagues first taught neurons to play the classic video game Pong in 2021. Doom, with its more complex environment, was a natural next step, he says, because “the Internet always asks, ‘Can it play Doom?’”

The subculture around the meme reflects the crucial role of play in science, says Mars Buttfield-Addison, a software developer and PhD candidate at the University of Tasmania in Hobart, Australia. By engaging in play and having fun, researchers are using the same kind of creativity needed to solve scientific problems. “Making something silly doesn’t take any less work than making something really technical,” she adds, and engaging in fun science can be a powerful motivator.

Games in science

Doom is not the only video game used in research. The hugely popular sandbox game Minecraft has also been used to develop and test AI models3, and the online multiplayer game World of Warcraft has been used to simulate disease outbreaks4.

But Doom is particularly appealing for research because, in 1997, its code was published online by John Carmack, one of its developers at id Software in Richardson, Texas. This allows people to adapt the game so that it works on other platforms or devices, says Buttfield-Addison. Doom also doesn’t require much storage space, she adds.

From bacteria to satellites

Lauren 'Ren' Ramlan, a biological engineer at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge, has integrated the game into her research. In 2023, during the first year of her doctoral programme, she used Escherichia coli bacteria to display a few frames of Doom. She attached a fluorescent protein to the bacterial cells that could be turned on or off, making them act like black and white pixels on a screen. She then translated and compressed the first few frames of Doom into black-and-white versions that matched the plate growing the cells.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel and inspiring use of a classic computer game, Doom, as a scientific tool across diverse fields including synthetic biology and AI research. This represents a creative technological breakthrough with broad implications for scientific innovation and interdisciplinary research, engaging both the scientific community and the public.
articles.personality_title: How the game Doom became a surprising tool for scientific research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Doom is a popular computer game first released in 1993. Although it began as entertainment, its source code was made public in 1997, allowing scientists to use it in their research.

**What happened** – Researchers have used Doom in many ways. For example, scientists in Australia taught living neurons grown on a silicon chip to play Doom. Other experiments have run Doom on bacteria and even a satellite. These projects build on a community that enjoys trying to run Doom on unusual devices.

**Impact** – Doom is useful for science because it is simple to change and does not need much memory. This makes it easy to use in different scientific experiments, such as studying brain cells or testing artificial intelligence. The fun and challenge of making Doom work on new platforms also helps researchers stay creative and motivated.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely continue using Doom and other games to explore new ideas in biology, computer science, and technology. These projects might help develop better AI or understand how living cells process information.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The classic game Doom has become a unique tool that helps scientists explore biology and technology in creative new ways.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment le jeu Doom est devenu un outil surprenant pour la recherche scientifique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Doom est un jeu vidéo populaire sorti en 1993. Bien qu’il ait commencé comme un divertissement, son code source a été rendu public en 1997, permettant aux scientifiques de l’utiliser dans leurs recherches.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont utilisé Doom de plusieurs manières. Par exemple, des scientifiques australiens ont appris à des neurones vivants cultivés sur une puce en silicium à jouer à Doom. D’autres expériences ont fait fonctionner Doom sur des bactéries et même un satellite. Ces projets s’appuient sur une communauté qui aime essayer de faire tourner Doom sur des appareils inhabituels.

**Impact** – Doom est utile pour la science car il est facile à modifier et ne nécessite pas beaucoup de mémoire. Cela le rend facile à utiliser dans différentes expériences scientifiques, comme étudier les cellules du cerveau ou tester l’intelligence artificielle. Le plaisir et le défi de faire fonctionner Doom sur de nouvelles plateformes aident aussi les chercheurs à rester créatifs et motivés.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront probablement à utiliser Doom et d’autres jeux pour explorer de nouvelles idées en biologie, informatique et technologie. Ces projets pourraient aider à développer une meilleure IA ou à comprendre comment les cellules vivantes traitent l’information.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le jeu classique Doom est devenu un outil unique qui aide les scientifiques à explorer la biologie et la technologie de manière créative.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo el juego Doom se convirtió en una herramienta sorprendente para la investigación científica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Doom es un juego de computadora popular lanzado en 1993. Aunque comenzó como entretenimiento, su código fuente se publicó en 1997, lo que permitió a los científicos usarlo en sus investigaciones.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores han usado Doom de muchas maneras. Por ejemplo, científicos en Australia enseñaron a neuronas vivas cultivadas en un chip de silicio a jugar Doom. Otros experimentos hicieron funcionar Doom en bacterias e incluso en un satélite. Estos proyectos forman parte de una comunidad que disfruta intentar ejecutar Doom en dispositivos inusuales.

**Impacto** – Doom es útil para la ciencia porque es fácil de modificar y no necesita mucha memoria. Esto lo hace sencillo de usar en diferentes experimentos científicos, como estudiar células cerebrales o probar inteligencia artificial. La diversión y el reto de hacer que Doom funcione en nuevas plataformas también ayuda a los investigadores a mantenerse creativos y motivados.

**Próximo paso** – Es probable que los científicos sigan usando Doom y otros juegos para explorar nuevas ideas en biología, informática y tecnología. Estos proyectos podrían ayudar a desarrollar mejores inteligencias artificiales o entender cómo las células vivas procesan información.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El juego clásico Doom se ha convertido en una herramienta única que ayuda a los científicos a explorar la biología y la tecnología de formas creativas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vintage computer screen glowing softly with pixelated black-and-white frames of the classic Doom game, surrounded by stylized, gently glowing neurons connected on a silicon chip and a petri dish with tiny, illuminated E. coli bacteria arranged like pixels, all set against a calm, simple background of natural earth tones.

articles.title: ‘Einstein‘ bot sharpens debate over AI in the classroom
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00764-wFaculty members are spending ever more time outmanoeuvring students who want to cut corners with artificial intelligence.
articles.content:
An AI tool named after Albert Einstein (pictured) was taken down shortly after it was released.Credit: GK History Images/Alamy

On 23 February, academics across the world took to social media to decry the death of education as we know it. The day before, a technology start-up company called Companion had released an artificial-intelligence platform that pledged to free students from tedious coursework.

Such a statement might not seem controversial at a time when AI tools exist for nearly everything, if not for the fact that the program, called Einstein, promised on its website to do so much more. The company said that students could grant the tool access to their account on a virtual learning environment, such as Canvas. Once they did that, Einstein could watch lectures, read course material, participate in discussions, complete quizzes, and write and submit homework — all with minimal oversight by the student themselves.

Companion chief executive Advait Paliwal told the technology news outlet CNET that Einstein “makes ChatGPT look like a toy”, whereas educators called it “a cheating app”, “evil” and “the ultimate brain smoothing machine”. Language on the tool’s website shifted after the backlash to downplay the AI’s capabilities, and by 26 February, the bot was no longer accessible after a ‘cease-and-desist’ demand. Paliwal told Times Higher Education that he would now “concentrate on promoting how the wider Companion AI can be used by students”. (Attempts by Nature to reach Paliwal received no reply.)

Game over

Einstein’s moment in the Sun might have been short, but it is part of a wider reckoning over how students should be educated today. AI tools are being marketed as time savers for teachers overburdened by administrative tasks, and yet some faculty members are instead spending more time on battling bad-faith uses involving students, resulting in a push to return to ‘de-digitized’ curricula that place less emphasis on computers.

“My first thought when I saw Einstein was ‘game over’,” says Lilian Edwards, a specialist in Internet law and technology policy at Newcastle University, UK, because circumventing it would require instructors “to rearrange [their] assessment strategy entirely”, which would involve substantial effort. “AI can certainly be useful,” she adds, but the majority of people she knows “think it’s driving a stake through the heart of conventional educational assessment”.

AI has lots of legitimate uses in academia — including writing code, translating texts and correcting grammar — and David Jurgens, a computer scientist at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor, says it’s nearly impossible to avoid in his field. As such, he often faces many of the same ethical quandaries as his students. Jurgens came across another AI, called Professor Feynman, which is essentially Einstein for academics: it promises to free them from the ‘busywork’ of reading and grading essays, responding to discussions and even the need to offer online office hours, by creating a ‘digital twin’ that mimics their voice, mannerisms and teaching style.

“You can imagine a nightmare situation where classes become AIs talking to AIs, with no people actually interacting,” he says.

Rather than adapting his assessments to AI platforms, Jurgens has engaged his students in thoughtful discussions in the classroom.

“Teachers are always going to have to spend time developing and updating their curriculums, and so I’ve tried to make it a more collaborative process,” he says. “It feels like a better use of my time, and as a result, I do see students being more aware that they’re only hurting themselves in the long term if they’re replacing themselves with these tools.”

More collaboration
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and timely debate about the impact of advanced AI tools on education, showcasing educators' proactive efforts to adapt teaching methods and promote ethical AI use. This reflects a meaningful societal challenge with broad implications for education systems worldwide, emphasizing collaboration and thoughtful integration of technology rather than uncritical adoption or rejection.
articles.personality_title: New AI tool sparks fresh debate on teaching and cheating in schools
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A new artificial intelligence (AI) tool named Einstein was created to help students by doing their schoolwork for them. It could watch lectures, read materials, join discussions, take quizzes, and even submit homework with little effort from the students.

**What happened** – Einstein was released by a company called Companion in February 2026 but was quickly taken down after teachers and experts called it a cheating tool. Students could link Einstein to their online school accounts, which worried many educators who felt it could harm learning. The company stopped the tool after receiving a legal warning.

**Impact** – This event shows how AI can challenge traditional education by making it easier for students to avoid learning. Teachers now face the hard task of changing how they test students so that AI tools don’t make cheating easier. Some educators are also trying to use AI in better ways, like helping with grading or translating, but they worry about losing real interaction between students and teachers.

**What's next step** – Schools and teachers will need to rethink how they design lessons and tests to keep learning fair and meaningful. Many believe working closely with students to understand AI’s role will help. Meanwhile, AI companies might focus on creating tools that support learning instead of replacing it.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The rise and fall of Einstein highlights the need for schools to adapt teaching methods as AI tools become more powerful and widespread.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel outil d’IA relance le débat sur l’enseignement et la triche à l’école
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Un nouvel outil d’intelligence artificielle (IA) nommé Einstein a été créé pour aider les élèves en réalisant leurs devoirs à leur place. Il pouvait regarder les cours, lire les documents, participer aux discussions, passer les quiz et même rendre les devoirs avec peu d’effort de la part des élèves.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Einstein a été lancé par une entreprise appelée Companion en février 2026, mais a rapidement été retiré après que les enseignants et les experts l’ont qualifié d’outil de triche. Les élèves pouvaient connecter Einstein à leurs comptes scolaires en ligne, ce qui a inquiété beaucoup d’enseignants craignant que cela nuise à l’apprentissage. L’entreprise a arrêté l’outil après avoir reçu une mise en demeure.

**Impact** – Cet événement montre comment l’IA peut remettre en question l’éducation traditionnelle en facilitant la triche. Les enseignants doivent maintenant modifier leurs méthodes d’évaluation pour que l’IA ne facilite pas la triche. Certains éducateurs essaient aussi d’utiliser l’IA de manière positive, comme pour corriger ou traduire, mais ils craignent de perdre le vrai contact entre élèves et professeurs.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les écoles et les enseignants devront repenser leurs cours et leurs évaluations pour préserver un apprentissage juste et efficace. Beaucoup pensent que collaborer avec les élèves pour comprendre le rôle de l’IA sera utile. Par ailleurs, les entreprises d’IA pourraient se concentrer sur la création d’outils qui soutiennent l’apprentissage plutôt que de le remplacer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La montée et la chute d’Einstein soulignent la nécessité pour les écoles d’adapter leurs méthodes d’enseignement face à la puissance croissante des outils d’IA.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva herramienta de IA reaviva el debate sobre enseñanza y trampas en la escuela
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Se creó una nueva herramienta de inteligencia artificial (IA) llamada Einstein para ayudar a los estudiantes haciendo sus tareas por ellos. Podía ver clases, leer materiales, participar en discusiones, hacer quizzes e incluso entregar la tarea con poco esfuerzo del estudiante.

**Qué pasó** – Einstein fue lanzada por una empresa llamada Companion en febrero de 2026, pero fue retirada rápidamente después de que profesores y expertos la calificaron como una herramienta para hacer trampa. Los estudiantes podían conectar Einstein a sus cuentas escolares en línea, lo que preocupó a muchos maestros que temían que dañara el aprendizaje. La empresa detuvo la herramienta tras recibir una orden legal.

**Impacto** – Este evento muestra cómo la IA puede desafiar la educación tradicional facilitando que los estudiantes eviten aprender. Los profesores ahora tienen la difícil tarea de cambiar cómo evalúan para que la IA no facilite la trampa. Algunos educadores también intentan usar la IA de forma positiva, como para corregir o traducir, pero temen perder la interacción real entre estudiantes y maestros.

**Próximo paso** – Las escuelas y maestros deberán repensar sus lecciones y exámenes para mantener el aprendizaje justo y significativo. Muchos creen que trabajar junto a los estudiantes para entender el papel de la IA ayudará. Mientras tanto, las empresas de IA podrían enfocarse en crear herramientas que apoyen el aprendizaje en lugar de reemplazarlo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El surgimiento y la retirada de Einstein muestran la necesidad de que las escuelas adapten sus métodos de enseñanza a medida que las herramientas de IA se vuelven más poderosas y comunes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of an open book transforming into a glowing, intricate network of gears and circuits shaped like a wise owl, symbolizing AI’s role in education, with a gentle hand gently closing the book to suggest thoughtful human guidance and collaboration, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft lighting.
